HEADLINE: Iranians block a street during a protest in Tehran, Iran on 9 January 2026
SOURCE: The Guardian

ARTICLE:

Iranians block a street during a protest in Tehran, Iran on 9 January 2026. At least 6,000 deaths have been verified by one human rights organisation, which is investigating more that 17,000 more. Photograph: MAHSA/Middle East Images/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenIranians block a street during a protest in Tehran, Iran on 9 January 2026. At least 6,000 deaths have been verified by one human rights organisation, which is investigating more that 17,000 more. Photograph: MAHSA/Middle East Images/AFP/Getty ImagesRights and freedomIranIran accused of ГўВҖВҳcampaign of revengeГўВҖВҷ as doctors arrested for treating protesters US state department calls for the release of all detained healthcare workers as at least one arrested surgeon reported to be at risk of executionSupported byAbout this contentDeepa ParentThu 29 Jan 2026 00.00 ESTLast modified on Thu 29 Jan 2026 14.21 ESTShareDoctors are being arrested in Iran for helping save the lives of some of the tens of thousands injured during IranГўВҖВҷs brutal crackdown on anti-regime protests, with at least one surgeon now at risk of being sentenced to death.The arrests and death sentence are part of a campaign of ГўВҖВңrevengeГўВҖВқ, say human rights groups, after healthcare workers and doctors refused to ignore the plight of badly injured protesters shot or stabbed at close range, and in some cases set up makeshift treatment centres.An Iranian surgeon, Alireza Golchini, 52, from the central city of Qazvin, has been charged with ГўВҖВңmoharebehГўВҖВқ (waging war against God), which can carry the death penalty, according to the Norway-based rights group Hengaw. The US state department yesterday called for his release.The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency, whose figures have been reliable during previous crackdowns, says it has verified more than 6,000 dead and has more than 17,000 more recorded deaths under investigation.0:46Bodies line the streets outside a morgue in Tehran as protests continue ГўВҖВ“ videoSpeaking to the Guardian, his cousin, Nima Golchini, who is based in Canada, said that Golchini was taken from his home on 10 January. ГўВҖВңHe was arrested in a violent manner in front of his wife and son, who is only 11. They beat him up so badly during arrest, they broke his arm, ribs and dragged him out of his home. My family is terrified.ГўВҖВқA few days before his arrest Golchini, who also treated protesters during the 2022 Woman, life, freedom protests, had posted a note on his social media, says Nima, sharing his number and asking injured patients to contact him for treatment.ГўВҖВңAll he did was his duty of saving lives as a medical doctor. He had sworn to save peopleГўВҖВҷs lives. How can any doctor not stand by his oath? I am worried not only for him, but also for other healthcare workers who have been arrested for simply standing by their sworn oath.ГўВҖВқIranian authorities have not publicly commented on GolchiniГўВҖВҷs detention, nor have they confirmed any charges against him. But IranГўВҖВҷs judiciary chief, Gholamhossein Mohseni Ejei, has urged authorities not to show any leniency towards protesters. ГўВҖВңWe should not remain silent in the face of those who seek to exploit the situation and disrupt the security and calm of the people,ГўВҖВқ he said.View image in fullscreenThe Iranian surgeon Alireza Golchini, who studied in London, was arrested and reportedly charged with ГўВҖВҳwaging war against God Photograph: suppliedGolchini is one of at least nine doctors and healthcare volunteers arrested over the past week, say rights groups and medics. According to the Norway-based Iran Human Rights (IHRNGO), security forces raided makeshift medical shelters as well as the homes of doctors and volunteers who treated injured protesters. It said there is no information now on the whereabouts or condition of those detained.Iran protest doctor: ГўВҖВҳIn one street, I saw blood pooled in a gutter with a trail stretching several metresГўВҖВҷ | AnonymousRead moreГўВҖВңThis appears to be a deliberate revenge campaign against doctors and medical staff who refuse to abandon the wounded,ГўВҖВқ said Hossein Raeesi, an Iranian human rights lawyer living in exile.IHRNGO also reported the arrest of a volunteer first responder who had turned his home into an improvised medical shelter. According to the source, he was detained on 14 January after security forces raided his house, where he had provided care to more than 20 injured protesters, two of whom later died.ГўВҖВңHe was taken away in an extremely brutal manner and was severely beaten,ГўВҖВқ a source told IHRNGO, adding that security forces smashed the windows of the house, destroyed the interior and severely damaged his car during the raid.At least 42,324 arrests have been made across the country with limited information on their fate, according to the US-based Human Rights Activists in Iran, which says the regime is putting pressure on medical networks as a means of reducing support for the injured.ГўВҖВңThis persecution of medical personnel is yet another dimension of the regimeГўВҖВҷs crimes against humanity,ГўВҖВқ said Mahmood Amiry-Moghaddam, IHRNGO director.A statement published yesterday by the US state department on X demanded the release of Golchini and ГўВҖВңall the brave doctors who have helped their fellow countrymenГўВҖВқ. It continued: ГўВҖВңPresident Trump has clearly stated that no executions should take place in Iran and that there will be consequences if the government takes such actions.ГўВҖВқExplore more on these topicsIranRights and freedomMiddle East and north AfricaProtestnewsShareReuse this contentMost viewedMost viewed

The language used is strongly critical of state violence and repressive government measures, which is consistent with left/liberal values emphasizing individual rights, freedom, and opposition to authoritarianism. The narrative notably foregrounds the perspective of victims and NGOs, which is a hallmark of left-leaning coverage on international human rights issues.","key_indicators":["Published by The Guardian (historically Left/Liberal)","Extensive, positive coverage of human rights groups","Sympathetic framing of protesters and detained doctors","Strong criticism of Iranian state repression","Focus on civil liberties and anti-authoritarianism"]}

The content of the article focuses heavily on human rights abuses by the Iranian regime, with clear sympathy for anti-regime protesters and detained healthcare workers, highlighting their mistreatment and the role of US and international human rights groups. The language used is strongly critical of state violence and repressive government measures, which is consistent with left/liberal values emphasizing individual rights, freedom, and opposition to authoritarianism. The narrative notably foregrounds the perspective of victims and NGOs, which is a hallmark of left-leaning coverage on international human rights issues.
